coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal functionalization of graphene quantum dots extracted from cellulose
coredata.dc:creator: Rabeya, Ramisha
Mahalingam, Savisha
Lau, Kam Sheng
Manap, Abreeza
Satgunam, Meenaloshini
Chia, Chin Hua
Akhtaruzzaman, Md.
coredata.dc:description: Functionalization is a promising approach to modify the physical and chemical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). However, the synthesis of functionalized GQDs (F-GQDs) is usually conducted with strong acids. Thus, the sustainable synthesis route of F-GQDs remains a challenge. This is important for suitable optimization of GQDs to be applied in sustainable photovoltaic applications, especially dye-sensitized solar cells owing to the strong attachment of functional group elements. This study presents a detailed study of optical, structural, and chemical changes that occurred in GQDs during the functionalization process by adding an ionic liquid, 1-ethyl-1-methylpyrrolidium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide via hydrothermal synthesis approach using an eco-friendly route comprising only cellulose and deionized (DI) water. The presence of ionic liquid provides fundamental elements (nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and sulfur (S)), which are added to GQDs producing F-GQDs. The optimum result shows that the 20 wt% N, F, S functionalized GQDs have the largest UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission. The F-GQDs also revealed a single crystalline hexagonal graphene-like honeycomb structure in transmission electron microscopy and increased roughness relatively from atomic force microscopy. Moreover, the Fourier transform infrared and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy have also confirmed the presence of C-N, C=S, C-F, and N-H functional groups in the F-GQDs produced.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose
Functionalization
GQDs
Hydrothermal
NFS-GQDs

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of GQDs. The hydrothermal process of extracting GQDs from cellulose for two different synthesis times have been illustrated.

Synthesis of NFS-GQDs. The hydrothermal process of extracting NFS-GQDs from cellulose has been illustrated. Three different weight percentages (10 wt%, 20 wt%, and 30 wt%) have been chosen for synthesis.
Synthesis technique of GQDs and NFS-GQDs. The hydrothermal synthesis process for both GQDs and NFS-GQDs have been depicted.

Luminescence of (a) 8 h synthesized GQDs (b) 24 h synthesized GQDs (a) 10 wt% NFS-GQDs (b) 20 wt% NFS-GQDs (c) 30 wt% NFS-GQDs. All the synthesized and F-GQDs solutions have been tested under UV light to determine the luminescence, as seen in the figure. The 24 h synthesized GQDs show a cloudy formation, whereas the 20 wt% NFS-GQDs relatively exhibited the brightest luminescence as can be observed.

Combined UV–vis plots of (a) synthesized GQDs. (b) F-GQDs The UV–vis plots of 8 h and 24 h synthesized GQDs and 10 wt%, 20 wt% and 30 wt% NFS-GQDs have been illustrated. 8 h synthesized GQDs show absorption at 284 nm, whereas 24 h synthesized GQDs show that at 285 nm. The highest absorption has been occupied by the 20 wt% NFS-GQDs comparatively, which is 287 nm.

Combined photon energy spectra of synthesized GQDs (b) Combined photon energy spectra of F-GQDs. The photon energy spectra of all the synthesized and functionalized GQDs have been illustrated. The bandgap of 24 h synthesized GQDs possessed a lower bandgap of 3.94 eV compared to the other synthesized GQDs and that of 20 wt% NFS-GQDs held a lower bandgap of 4.98 eV relative to the other F-GQDs.

Computed UV–Vis spectrum for synthesized (a) small size GQDs (b) medium size GQDs (c) large size GQDs with inserted chemical structures. The spectrum of large size GQDs relatively owns a strong correlation with that of the experimental sample.

Computed UV–vis spectrum of for (a) N-GQDs (b) N doped-GQDs (c) N, F doped GQDs (d) N, F, S doped GQDs with inserted chemical structures. The presence of N, F and S led to a slight red-shift relative to experimental result indicating that the GQDs are doped N, F and S.

Combined PL plots of (a) synthesized GQDs (b) F-GQDs. The PL emission of 8 h and 24 h synthesized GQDs and 10 wt%, 20 wt% and 30 wt%. NFS-GQDs have been illustrated. The 24 h synthesized GQDs exhibited blue light at 435 nm with higher PL intensity compared to that of 8 h synthesized GQDs. However, the 20 wt% NFS-GQDs exhibited brighter luminescence of blue light with even higher intensity indicating enhance PL performance for F-GQDs.

(a) Full XPS plot of synthesized GQDs (b-c) Corresponding deconvoluted peaks of C1s and O1s respectively (c) Full XPS plot of F-GQDs (d-i) Corresponding deconvoluted peaks of C1s, O1s, N1s, F1s, S1s respectively. The presence of C = C, C-O, C = O has been confirmed for the 24 h synthesized GQDs. The presence of these bonds also exists for F-GQDs including the bonding of N, F and S with GQDs, which comprises C-N, C = F, C-S-C and C-SO2 bonds. This suggests that successful doping of GQDs have taken place.

FTIR plots of (a) synthesized GQDs (b) F-GQDs. Both the synthesized and F-GQDs have absorption of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The common absorption C-O, C-H, C-O-C and OH have been identified for both the synthesized GQDs, but it is stronger for 24 h synthesized GQDs. Whereby, the F-GQDs exhibited that for C-N, C-F, C = S, N-H and COOH functional groups along with those obtained for GQDs. The absorption of each of the functional groups are relatively stronger for 20 wt% NFS-GQDs suggesting effective doping of GQDs.

TEM image of (a) synthesized GQDs (b) corresponding size distribution. The TEM analysis for 24 h synthesized GQDs has been illustrated. It exhibits a polycrystalline structure with the d-spacing of 0.50 nm corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite lattice.

TEM image of (a) F-GQDs. The TEM analysis for 20 wt% NFS-GQDs has been illustrated. It exhibits a hexagonal graphene-like honeycomb structure with 0.13 nm of d-spacing, which refers to the (1120) plane of graphene.

AFM images and corresponding data of (a) 8 h synthesized GQDs (b) 24 h synthesized GQDs (c) 10 wt% NFS-GQDs (d) 20 wt% NFS-GQDs (e) 30 wt% NFS-GQDs. The 24 h synthesized sample possessed 9 nm of height with greater roughness and grain size of 1.832 nm and 14.087 respectively, whereas the 20 wt% owns an average height of 80 nm with 20.367 nm of roughness and 14.087 nm of grain size. This suggests enhanced morphology of 20 wt% NFS-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal functionalization of graphene quantum dots extracted from cellulose

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Solar Energy Research Institute (SERI), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, Selangor 43600, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functionalization is a promising approach to modify the physical and chemical properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). However, the synthesis of functionalized GQDs (F-GQDs) is usually conducted with strong acids. Thus, the sustainable synthesis route of F-GQDs remains a challenge. This is important for suitable optimization of GQDs to be applied in sustainable photovoltaic applications, especially dye-sensitized solar cells owing to the strong attachment of functional group elements. This study presents a detailed study of optical, structural, and chemical changes that occurred in GQDs during the functionalization process by adding an ionic liquid, 1-ethyl-1-methylpyrrolidium bis(trifluoromethylsulfonyl)imide via hydrothermal synthesis approach using an eco-friendly route comprising only cellulose and deionized (DI) water. The presence of ionic liquid provides fundamental elements (nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and sulfur (S)), which are added to GQDs producing F-GQDs. The optimum result shows that the 20 wt% N, F, S functionalized GQDs have the largest UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence emission. The F-GQDs also revealed a single crystalline hexagonal graphene-like honeycomb structure in transmission electron microscopy and increased roughness relatively from atomic force microscopy. Moreover, the Fourier transform infrared and x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy have also confirmed the presence of C-N, C=S, C-F, and N-H functional groups in the F-GQDs produced.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen, fluorine, sulfur-based graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: One of the current focuses in the scientific community is graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Its remarkable optoelectronic properties, along with high photostability and biocompatibility, and low toxicity [1], followed by extensive applications ranging from biological to photovoltaic (PV) fields [2] make it a highly substantial material to be exploited further. Furthermore, the growth in the potential of GQDs for PV applications is escalating inexorably. GQDs have infinite Bohr diameter with non-zero bandgap and excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL). These properties are tunable by modifying the size, shape, surface chemistry, edge configurations, and chemical functionalization [3]. GQDs are well adaptable to chemical functionalization, which contributes significantly to PV applications like dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) [4], which can also effectively work in low-level lightings [5] and much better than silicon solar cells [6].

Functionalization in chemistry means adding new functions, features, and characteristics to modify surface chemistry [7]. The functionalization process in GQDs is a promising approach because the chemical adsorption of atoms or molecules prevents the destruction of the carbon network without substitutional impurities [7]. Functionalization in GQDs is important, as it aids in tuning the optical, physical, and chemical properties, which turns out to be beneficial for the resultant applications, mainly DSSCs. One of the vital causes can be due to the robust attachment of the functional group of GQDs with the functional group elements of the functionalizing solvent. This can eventually be expected to boost the dye loading at the photoanode interface, which leads to highly efficient DSSCs.

GQDs-based DSSC has been fabricated by Mahalingam et al. [8], where GQDs were assembled with dye in a mixture form to prepare titanium dioxide photoanode resulting in highly efficient DSSC relative to layer by layer form. Thus, photoanode layer is one of the crucial part of DSSCs, which demands an appropriate and porous surface structure for better DSSCs [9]. This can be acquired by applying functionalized GQDs in the preparation of photoanode. Kundu et al. [10] reported the synthesis of functionalized GQDs (F-GQDs) via microwave treatment of carbon nanotubes. The GQDs were co-doped with nitrogen (N), fluorine (F), and sulfur (S), which exist in the ionic liquid, {1-methyl-1-propylpiperidiniumbis (trifluoro methylsulfonyl) imide}. It enhanced the PL quantum yield by 70%, and the F-GQDs have an average size of approximately 2 nm. The XPS survey confirmed the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), N, F, and S, whereas the FTIR ratified the stretching vibration of –C-F, S = O, -C-S, -O-H/-N-H, and –C-S-C bonds.

Furthermore, cysteine-functionalized GQDs were synthesized via carbonization of citric acid, as reported by Tam and co [11]. It was applied for a fluorescence probe for mercury ions. Strong green fluorescence was observed, followed by uniform lateral size distribution and highly crystalline cysteine-functionalized GQDs. Besides, Hnayn et al. [12] reported the synthesis of diamine-functionalized GQDs obtained through oxidative fragmentation of graphene oxide. The preparation trailed aqueous 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) and hydrogen peroxide, including hydrochloric acid. The resultant F-GQDs possessed good colloidal stability with an average size of 4 nm, followed by emission of intense green-yellow light and PL quantum yield of 22% when excited at 375 nm.

Although GQDs can be functionable in terms of other distinct applications, their good adaptability to chemical functionalization also assists well in the effective development of the application. For example, Chen et al. [13] reported the synthesis of high-quality GQDs via novel precursor, 1,2,4- trihydroxy benzene applied for hydrogen evolution, which has been further developed by a multifunctional stage of Rh@GQDs using hydrolysis process. However, all these F-GQDs were synthesized using strong acids. These acids, or organic solvents affect their properties, as they are environmentally hazardous. Moreover, post-processing takes more time to remove the impurities, including the acid residues. These drawbacks hinder the development of applications with GQDs [14].

Accordingly, the environmentally-friendly synthesis of carbon-based materials and GQDs is essential compared to that prepared with acidic methods. For example, Chen et al. [15] reported the green synthesis of carbon nanodots produced from waste pollutants for bioimaging applications. Besides, Chen et al. [14] has also reported the green synthesis of GQDs using cotton cellulose, which produced a renewable resource for biological applications. On that note, the GQDs in this work have been synthesized in an environmentally friendly way via a hydrothermal route with only cellulose and water. However, taking the eco-friendly synthesis of GQDs into contemplation, their improved absorption and emission properties followed by controlled size are difficult to achieve.

Herein, we reported a new ionic liquid (1-ethyl-1 methylpyrrolidium bis(trifluoro methylsulfonyl)imide) used in the functionalization of GQDs, which consisting of three fundamental elements, N, F, and S. Other researchers have not used this ionic liquid have worked on its amalgamation with GQDs for property enhancement. The pure GQDs and functionalized F-GQDs (NFS-GQDs) had undergone optical, structural, and chemical characterizations. F-GQDs proved more superior performance comparatively. This can lead to the novel synthesis of GQDs, which will reveal their more hidden for future sustainable PV applications [16].

GQDs have been synthesized by combining 21 mg of cellulose and 25 mL of distilled water, and the mixture has undergone 30 min ultrasonication followed by hydrothermal synthesis method, which has been carried out for two different sets of time 8 h and 24 h. 24 h has been chosen to be the most appropriate synthesis time based on the results obtained from UV–vis characterization. Generally, the size of particles increases with an increase in hydrothermal synthesis duration as the reactants are adequately absorbed.

So, the synthesis taking place for 24 h results in agglomeration such as particle enlargement and change in morphology. Thus, synthesis time above 24 h has not been used to maintain the standard particle size of GQDs. Moreover, if a reaction is carried out beyond a certain limit, then there is a high possibility that the lattice structure will get ruptured. Therefore, all the synthesized GQDs samples have been centrifuged after the hydrothermal treatment. Fig. 1 illustrates the methodology of the entire procedure for hydrothermal synthesis of GQDs.

24 h synthesis time has been chosen for functionalizing GQDs. The solvent, 1-ethyl-1 methylpyrrolidium bis (trifluoromethylsulfonyl) imide has been added to the mixture of cellulose and water, and the same process is repeated involving ultrasonication and hydrothermal synthesis. Three different sets of contents of the functionalizing solvent have been selected, 10 wt% (2.1 mg), 20 wt% (4.2 mg) and 30 wt% (6.3 mg), and each of them has been individually assorted with the mixture of cellulose and water to obtain three different synthesized NFS-GQDs. The whole method is depicted in Fig. 2 . The overall preparation procedure of both the GQDs and NFS-GQDs has been illustrated in Fig. 3 .

The GQDs samples with and without functionalization have been exposed to various characterization tools. The optical characterization was carried out using ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35) and photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35). A computational calculation was carried out using a semi-empirical method known as the complete neglect differential overlap (CNDO/S) method to compute the electronic absorption spectra by using the proposed GQDs and F-GQDs structure to correlate with the experimental UV–vis results obtained from the as-synthesized samples. The structural characterization was conducted via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100F, 200 kV) and atomic force microscope (AFM) (Park Systems; NX 10). The chemical composition and bonding were determined using x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Kratos/Shimadzu; Axis Ultra DLD). The functional groups have been determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Agilent Technologies; Cary 630).

GQDs have been synthesized for two different sets of time, 8 h and 24 h, and were initially exposed to a UV lamp to observe their corresponding luminescence. The 24 h synthesized GQDs have a cloudy formation, as shown in Fig. 4 (b) compared to the 8 h synthesized sample shown in Fig. 4 (a). This might be due to the cellulose absorbing the water more strongly due to the longer synthesis duration.

The luminescence observed for the F-GQDs is depicted in Fig. 4 (c), (d), and (e), which are relatively more enhanced compared to the pure GQDs. The 20 wt% NFS-GQDs has been observed to possess clear, transparent blue luminescence as shown in Fig. 6 (b), 30 wt% in Fig. 6 (c) again showed a fade luminescence.

The UV–vis testing for both samples has been carried out to identify their subsequent absorption spectrum, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). As depicted in Fig. 5 (a), 8 h synthesized GQDs possessed the UV range of 284 nm, and 24 h showed the maximum absorbance in the UV region of 285 nm. The 24 h synthesized GQDs showed a maximum absorbance of approximately 2.0. From the absorption peaks obtained for both the samples, the transition states are determined to be п- п*, indicating that at these particular wavelengths, electrons get excited from п molecular orbital, which is the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to п*, lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). This is because the energy for this transition to occur is above 200 nm. This transition refers to the aromatic sp2 domain [17].

The UV–vis plots of F-GQDs are shown in Fig. 5 (b), where, rise in the amounts of functionalizing contents has led to greater absorbance. However, in the case of 30 wt% NFS-GQDs, the absorbance again dropped, implying that the functionalization rate has reached the maximum limit, which is evident with the luminescence observed for 20 and 30 wt%.

10 wt% NFS-GQDs exhibited absorption in the UV range of 278 nm, having an absorbance of 1.8, whereas 20 wt% holds the highest absorbance covering the range of 287 nm with an absorbance of nearly 2.45. The same range of wavelength is also covered by 30 wt%. However, no significant change in the absorption peaks has been identified for pure GQDs and F-GQDs.

According to the absorption wavelengths they covered based on the functionalization, n-п* transition occurs [18,19]. Any wavelength can be considered above 200 nm, including the maximum possible range, but it varies based on the conjugation of the particles. Such transition occurs in materials containing carbonyl compounds, unsaturated compounds, and heteroatoms [20]. The materials comprise three fundamental heteroatoms, N, F, and S; thus, this transition is expected.

Fig. 6 (a) and (b) illustrate the Tauc plot of both pure and F-GQDs. The energy bandgap of 8 h synthesized GQDs is 4.05 eV, and 24 h is 3.94 eV. Thus, greater synthesis time of the GQDs lowers the bandgap. 10 wt% NFS-GQDs exhibits a bandgap of around 5.0 eV, but the peak is negligible, whereas 20 wt% shows a bandgap of 4.98 eV and 30 wt% shows 5.58 eV.

When electrons are excited from the valence band to the conduction band, there are some levels where the electrons reach. When they lose some energies, they fall back to the original state, and thus the energy significantly decreases. The Sooner the electrons fall back to the ground state, indicating loss of energy, the poorer absorption capability is observed for 8 h synthesized GQDs and 10 wt% GQDs. A decrease in energy leads to an increase in the wavelength of light, as energy is inversely proportional to wavelength according to the formula, Eg = hc/λ. Eventually, both the pure GQDs and NFS-GQDs have absorbed photon energy corresponding to blue light.

Fig. 7 shows the computational simulated electronic adsorption spectrum for three different sizes of GQDs structure, which can estimate the theoretical size of the GQDs. Large-size GQDs show a similar spectrum compared with the experimental results based on the computed spectrum. Therefore, the proposed doped GQDs structure will be generated using the large-size GQDs structure. Furthermore, the computational simulated UV–Vis spectrum for doped GQDs samples, which can estimate the effect of functionalized elements in the GQDs was shown in Fig. 8 . The presence of heteroatoms (N, F, S) will cause a redshift in the UV–Vis spectrum, comparable with the experimental UV–Vis results observed in this study. Based on the experimental results, the NFS functionalized GQDs exhibit a slight redshift, suggesting that the GQDs are functionalized with N, F, and S, referring to the computational results.

Fig. 9 shows the PL luminescence for the pure GQDs and F-GQDs. Fig. 9 (a) shows the PL plots of 8 h and 24 h synthesized GQDs. Both the samples depicted the PL emission of blue light in the range of 435 nm to 440 nm, corresponding to the emission of blue light [19]. However, the 24 h sample shows an enhanced PL performance possessing the highest PL intensity and stronger photon emission. Electrons are excited by both the samples at 350 nm, although their emission energies are different. For 8 h sample, it can be observed that the wavelength at which the electron loses energy and comes back to a lower energy level or the relaxation state is smaller compared to that for the 24 h sample.

Moreover, it takes longer for the 8 h sample to reach the lower energy state and release the energy as light, which leads to a significant increase in energy resulting in greater shifting of the peak followed by emission at a longer wavelength. Although, the PL intensity is lower than the 24 h sample because the energy required for the electron to reach the higher state for the subsequent emission is lost quickly. Nevertheless, the 24 h sample excites the electron at 350 nm. It then takes a longer time to reach the higher energy level for what it falls to the lower energy state quickly compared to the 8 h sample stronger emission and greater PL intensity.

Therefore, from the hypothesis discussed above, 24 h sample has been considered the optimum synthesis time for GQDs, the functionalization was carried out by utilizing this synthesis time. Higher intensity resembles low defect density. So, all the functionalized samples (10 wt%, 20 wt%, and 30 wt%) have undergone 24 h synthesis, and their subsequent PL plots have been illustrated in Fig. 9 (b). The 20 wt% sample has occupied the highest PL intensity. Doping of GQDs with heteroatoms can alter the surface states of GQDs [16]. However, further increase in the rate of functionalization has led to a drop in intensity, indicating that 20 wt% can be considered the maximum functionalization amount for 24 h synthesized GQD. For 10 wt% sample, the peak is broader than 20 wt% and 30 wt%, denoting greater surface defects.

After excitation at 350 nm, electrons move to a higher energy level. Before relaxation, it stays longer at that level. While falling back to the lower energy level, it releases energy at a slower pace, which corresponds to the significant increase in energy followed by emission at a longer wavelength, but lowers PL intensity as it cannot sustain energy for a longer period compared to 20 wt% and 30 wt% samples. The PL emission of 20 wt% and 30 wt% samples are stronger as electrons lose energy quickly during the second emission leading to the electron falling to the lower energy level faster and emitting light at higher intensity, although less shifting due to shorter wavelength.

The emission for both 20 wt% and 30 wt% takes place at the same wavelength illuminating blue light. However, the PL intensity of 20 wt% is greater as the excitation occurs at a higher intensity and electron remains at a higher energy state for a longer time and releases energy faster, falling back quickly to the lower energy state leading to higher emission of blue light at a higher intensity as more energy could be reserved for emission.

Therefore, the functionalized sample emitting light at the highest intensity is said to have possessed the strongest PL characteristics, which is evident in the case of 20 wt% sample. However, 30 wt% NFS-GQDs again drop to lower intensity denoting that further increase in functionalization contents result in a negligible change in the emission properties as can be correlated with the other properties discussed above.

The chemical composition present in the pure and F-GQDs have been identified from XPS characterization as illustrated in Fig. 10 (a), (b) and (c) for 24 h synthesized GQDs and (d), (e), (f), (g), (h) and (i) for 30 wt% NFS-GQDs containing the XPS peaks and their corresponding deconvoluted peaks respectively. Fig. 10 (a) shows the full spectrum of 24 h synthesized GQDs, where higher contents of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) functional groups have been confirmed due to the presence of their 1 s peaks. Fig. 9 (b) and (c) depict the deconvoluted peaks of C and O, respectively.

Fig. 10 (b) shows the deconvoluted peaks of C, where a peak around 285 eV is observed, indicating a C = C bond. The peak observed within 288 eV to 290 eV corresponds to the C = O bond [21]. Fig. 10 (c) shows the deconvoluted peak of O. Three prominent peaks are observed around 529.5 eV, 532 eV, and 533.5 eV, where the peaks around 529.5 eV and 532 eV refer to the C = O bond and that around 533.5 eV belongs to the C-O bond [22]. The full spectrum of 30 wt% NFS-GQDs is illustrated in Fig. 10 (d). The significant peaks of C1s and O1s have been determined, and their corresponding deconvoluted peaks are depicted in Fig. 10 (e) and (f), respectively.

GQDs have been confirmed due to the C = C group identified at the peak around 285 eV as observed in the case of 24 h synthesized GQDs and the C = O bond referring to the peak between 288 eV and 290 eV. Moreover, the black peak observed around 286 eV denotes the presence of the C-N bond [23], confirming the functionalization of GQDs. The peaks of O1s have been demonstrated in Fig. 10 (f), where four significant peaks have been observed around 529 eV, 531 eV, 532 eV, and 533.5 eV, which also correspond to the functional groups attained in 24 h synthesized GQDs (C = O and C-O) bonds. However, it is noteworthy that the presence of the three fundamental groups, N, F, and S, in the functionalizing solvent has also been confirmed, as demonstrated in Fig. 10 (g), (h), and (i), respectively.

In Fig. 10 (g), the first significant peak is noticed at 400 eV, which denotes pyrrolic N, and that above 402 eV defines graphitic N, as evident from the peak of N-GQDs [24]. Besides, a C-N bond is observed here around 403 eV, denoted by the small blue peak [23]. In Fig. 12 (h), the peak at 686 eV refers to a semi-ionic C-F bond. Whereas, at 688 eV specifies covalent C-F bond indicating the functionalization of GQDs, as evident from the XPS plot of F1s in F-GQDs [25]. The peak observed around 168 eV in Fig. 10 (i) corresponds to the C-S-C bond at 170 eV signifies the C-SO2 bond [26]. Hence, this bonding information leads to the corroboration of GQDs being functionalized with the ionic solvent containing N, F, S, where each element and compound has a particular interaction with GQDs.

The chemical structure characterizations of functionalized and non-functionalized GQDs undergoing hydrothermal and microwave synthesis have been conducted via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The FTIR plots of 8 h and 24 h synthesized GQDs have been illustrated in Fig. 11 (a). As per the depiction, both pure and F-GQDs GQDs exhibit the absorption of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. The peak observed nearly at 3340 cm−1 corresponds to the OH group [27], whose presence is more obvious in 24 h synthesized GQDs relative to 8 h GQDs. The peak around 1215 cm−1 corresponds to C-O stretching vibration [28] observable for 8 h and 24 h. The peak at 2896 cm−1 denotes the C-H stretching vibration for 24 h synthesized GQDs, which is almost negligible for 8 h synthesized GQDs. However, the C-O-C stretching vibration is observed for 8 h synthesized GQDs at 790 cm−1. The 24 h synthesized GQDs show no visible peak for C-O-C stretching vibration, indicating that the GQDs sample has been partially reduced [27].

As depicted in Fig. 11 (b) for NFS-GQDs, the formation of GQDs can be well confirmed from the peaks obtained for 10 wt% and 20 wt%. However, 30 wt% hardly showed any peak for the carboxyl group present in GQDs, which is evident that 30 wt% is not the optimum amount for NFS to be associated with GQDs. Also, the –OH bond could be identified for 10 wt% and 20 wt% NFS-GQDs, around 2900 cm−1 [29], whereas it was found to be negligible for 30 wt% samples. However, the C = S bond is common and obvious for all the samples, as denoted by the peak at nearly the absorption band of 1110 cm−1, but the peak is not weaker for 30 wt% [30]. Additionally, the peak around 1408 cm−1 refers to the bending vibration of N-H [31]. These indicate that the bond between the heteroatoms and the element from GQDs functional group is not stronger enough in the case of 30 wt%, which again concludes that it is not the optimum concentration for GQDs. The peaks observed at 1600–1630 cm−1 refers to the COOH functional groups [22] presence in the 10 wt% and 20 wt% NFS-GQDs samples. For all three NFS-GQDs, the element, N, has been confirmed via the peaks acquired at 1350 cm−1, belonging to the C-N bond. Moreover, the absorption band around 1030 cm−1 refers to the C-F bond [32]. Therefore, the overall FTIR spectrum of NFS-GQDs suggests successful doping of GQDs by NFS, as correlated with the XPS results.

The morphology of GQDs and NFS-GQDs have been determined by TEM analysis in the scale of 100 nm along with the inset containing selected area diffraction (SAED) pattern as shown in Fig. 12 (a) and its corresponding size distribution graph in Fig. 12 (b). Here, the 24 h synthesized GQDs showed a graphene structure with an average size of less than 100 nm, usually from GQDs [33]. It exhibited a circular shape followed by an average size of approximately 68 nm. The SAED pattern is given in Fig. 12 (c), which shows four diffraction rings. This resembles that the 24 h synthesized GQDs possessed a polycrystalline structure formed by several crystalline structures, followed by their orientations in different directions. Hence, the diffraction takes place in different pathways leading to the formation of circular rings. The average d-spacing (Å) of the sample has been determined to be 0.50 nm, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite lattice [34].

The TEM analysis of 20 wt% NFS-GQDs have been represented in Fig. 13 (a), (b), and (c). Although it owns an average size of 32 nm approximately, nearly half of the non-functionalized GQDs, the SAED pattern reflects a single crystalline hexagonal structure, which is the tangible structure of GQDs. Also, here the SAED pattern shows four hexagonal diffraction patterns indicating that they are single crystalline, as there are single dots positioned on the exact alignment with discrete spots. Furthermore, all the particles are in the same order towards the edges. Hence, the diffraction would be particularly at one point. The average d-spacing has been determined to be 0.13 nm, which can be referred to as the (1120) lattice plane of graphene [35], as it appears as a good crystalline graphene-like honeycomb structure [36].

The morphological structure of the pure and F-GQDs has also been investigated using the AFM characterization tool. 24 h synthesized sample and 20 wt% functionalized GQDs have demonstrated better morphology. All the figures of the AFM analysis have been demonstrated in Fig. 14 (a-e). As illustrated in Fig. 14 (a) for 8 h synthesized sample, an average height of 3.518 nm has been determined, and the roughness was found out to be 0.483 nm.

However, the 24 h synthesized GQDs exhibited a better morphology, as depicted in Fig. 14 (b). It possessed an average height of 5.857 nm, which is greater than that of the 8 h sample, and the roughness was found to be 1.199 nm. Greater roughness leads to stronger morphology [37], and thus it can be confirmed from this result.

Since 24 h synthesized GQDs showed an improved morphology, this synthesis time has been chosen to be relatively optimum amongst the others and utilized for further synthesis with functionalizing contents (10 wt%, 20 wt%, and 30 wt%). Their corresponding AFM images have been shown in Fig. 14 (c), (d), and (e), respectively

The average height was observed to be 10.212 nm for 10 wt% NFS-GQDs as shown in Fig. 14 (c) trailed by 2.509 nm of roughness. The 20 wt% NFS-GQDs sample in Fig. 14 (d) possessed the highest average height of 68.763 nm, followed by 8.112 nm of roughness. Hence 20 wt% NFS-GQDs hold a satisfactory growth. On the other hand, the average height and roughness of 30 wt% NFS-GQDs in Fig. 14 (e) reduced to 13.136 nm and 1.177 nm.

Thus, 20 wt% NFS-GQDs exhibits the best morphology relatively with the highest average height and roughness representing the expansive surface area. Nonetheless, 30 wt% NFS-GQD again shows poor morphology compared to the 20 wt% sample. This confirms further increment of functionalizing contents with cellulose and water above 20 wt% results in performance drop in the overall sample. Hence, 20 wt% of NFS is considered to be the ideal amount for the suitable functionalization of GQDs.

The F-GQDs synthesized using cellulose and 1-ethyl-1 methylpyrrolidium bis (trifluoromethylsulfonyl) imide ionic liquid via hydrothermal synthesis had been firstly reported in this work. The synthesized F-GQDs show improved UV absorption at 287 nm and PL emission at 435 nm (blue luminescence) compared to GQDs. Furthermore, the optimum content of the functionalization is 20 wt% NFS-GQDs which exhibit structural and chemical properties as a single-crystalline hexagonal honeycomb structure of graphene correlated with 0.13 nm of d-spacing, which corresponds to the (1120) plane of graphene; and the significant bonding between the functional group elements of GQDs and NFS, which include C-N, C-F, C = S, C-S-C and N-H bonds. The F-GQDs formation mechanism suggested that N, F, S elements in the ionic liquid act as a source to functionalize the GQDs during the hydrothermal synthesis process.

However, its potential in preparation for photoanodes by GQDs and dye co-sensitization has not yet been exploited for DSSC. Hence, the NFS-GQDs synthesized in this work is expected to contribute to relatively high efficient DSSC than only GQDs-based DSSC. Furthermore, the capacity of GQDs is explored involving the study of its interaction with the new material being associated. Hence, the compatibility of GQDs with functionalization techniques can be made more good use of in future for effective photoelectrode preparation in sustainable DSSC application. This will eventually keep GQDs as one of the most focused topics in the scientific community, and their widespread applications will be highly encouraged for PVs especially.

Ramisha Rabeya: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation. Savisha Mahalingam: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Lau Kam Sheng: Conceptualization, Resources, Validation, Software, Validation. Abreeza Manap: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Meenaloshini Satgunam: Project administration, Supervision. Chin Hua Chia: Project administration, Supervision, Resources. Md. Akhtaruzzaman: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Ministry of Higher Education (MoHE) for funding this study under the Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) of Grant No. FRGS/1/2019/STG07/UNITEN/02/3 and Institute of Sustainable Energy, Universiti Tenaga Nasional (UNITEN).
